div.p: I want to reason more on the spirit of man;1 for I am dwelling on the body and spirit of man2—on the subject of the dead. I take my ring from my finger and liken it unto the mind of man—the immortal part,3 because it has no beginning. Suppose you cut it in two; then it has a beginning and an end; but join it again, and it continues one eternal round.4 So with the spirit of man. As the Lord liveth, if it had a beginning, it will have an end. All the fools and learned and wise men from the beginning of creation,5 who say that the spirit of man had a beginning, prove that it must have an end; and if that doctrine is true, then the doctrine of annihilation would be true. But if I am right, I might with boldness proclaim from the house-tops6 that God never had the power to create the spirit of man7 at all. God himself could not create himself.

Intelligence is eternal8 and exists upon a self-existent principle. It is a spirita from age to age,9 and there is no creation about it. All the minds and spirits that God ever sent into the world are susceptible of enlargement.10
div.div: The Power to Advance in Knowledge
div.p: The first principles of man are self-existent with God. God himself, finding he was in the midst of spirits and glory,11 because he was more intelligent,12 saw proper to institute laws whereby the rest could have a privilege to advance like himself.13 The relationship we have with God places us in a situation to advance in knowledge.14 He has power to institute laws to instruct the weaker intelligences,15 that they may be exalted with himself,16 so that they might have one glory upon another,17 and all that knowledge, power, glory,18 and intelligence, which is requisite in order to save them in the world of spirits.19,b

a“A spirit from age to age”—not “spirit from age to age”; but “a spirit,” that is, an entity, a person, an individual. This paragraph in the Prophet’s remarks may well be taken as an interpretation of Doc. and Cov., Sec. 93:29.—Note by Elder B. H. Roberts.

b“Behold this is my work and my glory—to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man.”—(The Lord to Moses, Book of Moses, chapt. 1:39, Pearl of Great Price)—that is, “to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man, as man. The passage has reference doubtless to man as composed of spirit and body—a proper “soul” (see Doc. and Cov. Sec. 88:15-16)—“For the spirit and the body is the soul of man; and the resurrection of the dead is the redemption of the soul.” In other words, the “work” and the “glory” of God are achieved in bringing to pass the “immortality and eternal life of man,” as man, in the eternal union of the spirit and body of man through the resurrection through the redemption of the soul. This
